name: Oklo Inc.

profile.description: Oklo Inc. develops advanced fission power plants to provide clean, reliable, affordable energy at scale. The company's Aurora Powerhouse is a liquid metal-cooled reactor designed to produce between 15 and 75 megawatts of electricity. Oklo is also commercializing nuclear fuel recycling and fuel fabrication technology that can convert used nuclear fuel into usable fuel for its reactors. Founded in 2013 and headquartered in Santa Clara, California.

innovation.label: STRONG
innovation.analysis: Track Record (2/3): Oklo has developed the Aurora Powerhouse — a liquid metal-cooled advanced fission reactor producing 15-75 MW — from concept to physical groundbreaking at Idaho National Laboratory over 13 years of focused execution [1]. The DOE approved the Safety Design Strategy for the Aurora Fuel Fabrication Facility, and the Tennessee Advanced Fuel Center for nuclear fuel recycling demonstrates genuine expansion into adjacent technology domains. The hiring of Pat Schweiger as CTO, a 40-year veteran of advanced fission systems including the Fast Flux Test Facility, strengthens technical credibility [2]. AI Deployment (1/2): Oklo has partnered with NVIDIA for AI-nuclear integration, positioning its reactors specifically for AI data center power needs [2]. The Meta Aurora-Ohio prepayment agreement for up to 1.2 GW with an initial 150 MW phase targeted around 2030 validates AI-driven commercial demand [1]. This is AI-adjacent infrastructure rather than core AI product development. Market Share (1/2): Oklo is a first-mover in advanced small modular fission with landmark commercial agreements — the 12 GW Switch deal and Meta partnership signal emerging market leadership in a nascent category where no competitor has reached commercial deployment [3]. Pipeline (2/2): Multiple projects with defined timelines create a credible development funnel: Aurora-INL under DOE authorization, Aurora-Ohio with Meta under NRC pathway, Aurora Eielson (military), Groves facility, and the Tennessee Advanced Fuel Center [1][3]. R&D Intensity (1/1): Nuclear reactor development is inherently R&D-intensive — the $51.2M Q1 2026 operating loss and $350-450M 2026 capex guidance reflect massive investment relative to a 215-person pre-revenue company [1].

inspiration.label: STRONG
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (3/4): Oklo's mission to provide 'clean, reliable, affordable fission power to the world' is specific, aspirational, and tied to decarbonization — a cause larger than the company itself [2]. The mission has been pursued consistently for 13 years through regulatory setbacks including an initial NRC license denial, pre-revenue operations, and leadership transitions, demonstrating genuine conviction that transcends quarterly results. Community Impact (1/2): CEO Jacob DeWitte's appointment to the President's Council of Advisors on Science and Technology (PCAST) in March 2026 demonstrates broader societal influence beyond the company [2]. Oklo's fuel recycling technology addresses the nuclear waste problem, converting spent fuel that would otherwise sit in containment for decades into usable reactor fuel — a genuine environmental contribution. Employee Retention (1/2): Maintaining 215 employees at a pre-revenue startup over 13 years suggests strong mission-driven retention [2]. The nuclear engineering talent market is highly competitive, and Oklo has attracted personnel from GE, Sandia National Labs, and the Naval reactor program, plus CTO Pat Schweiger's 40-year fission career — indicating the mission resonates with top technical talent. Insider Buying (2/2): CEO Jacob DeWitte co-founded Oklo in 2013 and has remained through the full pre-revenue journey, now serving as Chairman after Sam Altman's April 2025 transition to avoid OpenAI conflicts of interest [2]. DeWitte's PCAST appointment and sustained commitment through years without revenue signal deep personal alignment.

infrastructure.label: AT RISK
infrastructure.analysis: Oklo is a pre-revenue company, and its infrastructure metrics reflect early-stage development rather than operating business fundamentals. Q1 2026 net loss was $33.1M with $51.2M operating loss, and the company has zero commercial revenue [1]. D/E of 0.10% is essentially debt-free — only $2.6M in total debt against $2.21B in cash and marketable securities, providing approximately $2.6B in total liquidity [1][4]. This fortress balance sheet supports years of development runway with $350-450M in 2026 capex guidance and $80-100M operating cash burn. ROE of -8.87% and profit margin of 0% reflect pre-revenue operations [4]. Forward P/E of -73.37 is negative, with first revenue not expected until 2027 and profitability projected in the early 2030s. The absence of revenue, earnings, and positive margins mechanically produces a low infrastructure score, though the balance sheet strength significantly mitigates near-term financial risk. Liquidity floor cleared: 13.4M avg volume x $61.17 = $819.1M daily turnover [4].

ai_resilience.label: LOW RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (8/9): Nuclear power generation is physical infrastructure that AI fundamentally cannot replace — fission reactors require precision engineering, regulatory compliance, physical fuel handling, and on-site operations entirely outside the digital domain [3]. More critically, AI dramatically increases electricity demand through data center proliferation, making Oklo a direct structural beneficiary of AI adoption. Every new AI training cluster and hyperscale facility requires reliable baseload power, and nuclear provides the carbon-free, 24/7 generation that intermittent renewables cannot match. Oklo's Aurora Powerhouse is specifically designed for data center co-location, and the company's regulatory and engineering moats — NRC licensing, DOE fuel approvals, nuclear safety expertise — create barriers no software competitor can breach [1][3]. AI Opportunity (1/1): Oklo is actively building AI-enabling infrastructure with specific commercial validation: the NVIDIA partnership for AI-nuclear integration, the Meta Aurora-Ohio prepayment agreement for up to 1.2 GW of power, and the 12 GW Switch agreement all demonstrate that AI companies are choosing Oklo as a power source [2][3]. The company is not merely AI-resilient — it is positioned as critical AI infrastructure, with Sam Altman's prior involvement reinforcing the AI-nuclear connection.

infinite_game.summary: Oklo demonstrates strong infinite-game characteristics through its 13-year commitment to a cause that transcends quarterly results, willingness to endure extended pre-revenue operations and an NRC license denial, and a mission tied to generational energy transformation. CEO DeWitte's sustained leadership from founding through PCAST appointment reflects genuine long-term orientation. The company's pre-revenue status limits evidence on some dimensions, but the foundational commitment to nuclear power as decades-long infrastructure is inherently infinite-minded.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (3/3): Oklo's cause — providing clean, reliable, affordable fission power to the world — is genuinely just and forward-looking [2]. It serves humanity's need for carbon-free baseload energy, a challenge that will outlast any individual or company. The 13-year pre-revenue journey, including persisting through an NRC license denial and pivoting the licensing approach, demonstrates that the founders are willing to sacrifice near-term financial returns for a cause they believe transcends the company itself. Nuclear power plants operate for 40-60 years — building this technology is inherently a commitment to a future the founders may not fully see.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (1/2): With 215 employees, Oklo operates at a scale where trust can be personal and direct. The team includes talent recruited from elite nuclear institutions — GE, Sandia National Labs, Naval reactors, plus CTO Pat Schweiger with 40 years of advanced fission experience — suggesting that talented engineers chose Oklo's mission over more established employers [2]. The Sam Altman board transition was handled transparently to address conflicts of interest, showing governance maturity. However, limited public data on internal culture, employee satisfaction, or organizational trust indicators prevents stronger scoring.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (1/1): Oklo operates alongside formidable competitors — NuScale, TerraPower (backed by Bill Gates), X-energy, and traditional nuclear giants like Westinghouse and GE Hitachi. Rather than positioning against these rivals, Oklo focuses on its unique technology approach (liquid metal cooling, fuel recycling) and specific market niche (modular deployments for data centers) [2][3]. The company's partnerships with NVIDIA, Meta, and Switch demonstrate differentiated capability rather than head-to-head rivalry, reflecting a learn-and-improve posture consistent with infinite-game thinking.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (1/2): Oklo has demonstrated meaningful strategic adaptation within its nuclear focus. After the NRC denied its initial micro-reactor license application, the company pivoted to the Aurora Powerhouse platform with a different licensing approach, expanded power range (15-75 MW), and dual-track regulatory strategy — DOE authorization for first-of-a-kind assets and NRC pathway for broader commercial deployment [1][3]. The addition of nuclear fuel recycling technology and the Tennessee Advanced Fuel Center represent strategic expansion. However, the company has not yet faced an existential market shift requiring fundamental reinvention.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (1/2): Operating for 13 years as a pre-revenue company in the heavily regulated nuclear industry requires genuine courage — Oklo sustained $51.2M in Q1 2026 operating losses while building technology that may not generate revenue until 2027 or later [1]. CEO DeWitte chose nuclear power, one of the most capital-intensive, regulated, and politically complex industries, as a startup opportunity. The $2.6B in liquidity demonstrates market conviction, and the guidance for $350-450M in 2026 capex shows willingness to invest aggressively. However, the courage to lead is partially untested — the company has not yet faced the operational challenges of actual power generation.

investment_thesis: Oklo is a pre-revenue pure play on the convergence of advanced nuclear power and AI-driven electricity demand. The Aurora Powerhouse — a liquid metal-cooled fission reactor producing 15-75 MW — has progressed from concept to physical groundbreaking at Idaho National Laboratory, with a dual-track regulatory strategy pursuing DOE authorization for first-of-a-kind assets and NRC pathway for broader commercial deployment [1][3]. Commercial validation is exceptional for a pre-revenue company: a 12 GW Switch agreement, the Meta Aurora-Ohio prepayment for up to 1.2 GW, and NVIDIA collaboration for AI-nuclear integration demonstrate that major tech companies are choosing Oklo as their clean energy partner [2][3]. With $2.6B in liquidity and essentially zero debt (D/E of 0.10%), Oklo has substantial runway to fund $350-450M in 2026 capex through first revenue projected for 2027 [1]. The Inspire Insight score of 48 with 20 positive ESG attributions and zero negatives reflects strong values alignment. AI resilience scores 9/10 — nuclear power is physical infrastructure that AI cannot replace while AI dramatically increases Oklo's addressable market.
thesis_continued: The bear case centers on execution risk, extended pre-revenue timeline, and extreme valuation. Oklo trades at a $10.6B market cap with zero revenue, negative earnings (-$0.84 EPS), and no commercial reactor in operation — the entire valuation rests on future technology deployment and regulatory approvals that remain uncertain [4]. Q1 2026 net loss was $33.1M with $51.2M operating loss, and the company guides $80-100M in 2026 operating cash burn plus $350-450M capex [1]. First revenue is not expected before 2027, with profitability projected in the early 2030s, creating a multi-year speculative window. The NRC licensing pathway for advanced reactors is largely untested, and the prior license denial demonstrates regulatory risk is real, not theoretical. Competition from well-funded rivals including TerraPower, NuScale, and X-energy could compress Oklo's first-mover advantage. Infrastructure scores 2/10 mechanically due to the complete absence of revenue and earnings — this accurately reflects that Oklo is a development-stage company whose investment case rests entirely on vision rather than demonstrated financial performance. Investors are paying for generational infrastructure that may take a decade to validate.
key_catalysts: Aurora Powerhouse groundbreaking at Idaho National Laboratory with DOE-approved Safety Design Strategy, dual-track regulatory approach (DOE authorization plus NRC commercial pathway), and CTO Pat Schweiger bringing 40 years of advanced fission expertise [1][2]

Landmark commercial agreements — Meta Aurora-Ohio prepayment for up to 1.2 GW (initial 150 MW phase ~2030), 12 GW Switch deal, and NVIDIA AI-nuclear partnership — validate demand for modular nuclear at data center scale [2][3]
$2.6B in total liquidity with essentially zero debt provides multi-year development runway, fully funding $350-450M 2026 capex and $80-100M operating cash burn through first revenue [1]
key_risks: Pre-revenue company trading at $10.6B market cap with zero commercial operations — entire valuation depends on successful NRC/DOE licensing, reactor construction, and power generation that remain years away, with prior NRC license denial demonstrating regulatory risk [1][4]

Q1 2026 operating loss of $51.2M with $350-450M capex guidance and first revenue not expected until 2027, profitability early 2030s — extended timeline creates dilution risk and sentiment sensitivity [1]

Advanced nuclear regulatory pathway is largely untested at commercial scale, while well-funded competitors (TerraPower, NuScale, X-energy, Westinghouse) pursue parallel strategies that could compress Oklo's first-mover advantage [2][3]
